intro: The pivotal question between OpenX and Fyber is how much of your setup stays portable: OpenX carries low lock-in and Fyber carries high lock-in. OpenX is programmatic ad exchange and supply-side platform for publishers. Fyber is mobile ad mediation and in-app bidding platform (Digital Turbine). Pick OpenX when the job is large publishers wanting another demand source in their header bidding wrapper and you accept low lock-in; pick Fyber when it is existing fyber customers and publishers already using digital turbine's dt exchange and you accept high lock-in. The data we have shows OpenX at opaque pricing and product information with average developer experience, and Fyber at opaque pricing and product information with average developer experience. The honest trade-off: neither is universal — OpenX is a poor fit for mobile-only app publishers needing sdk-first tooling and app-specific demand, and Fyber is a poor fit for new publishers evaluating mediation in 2026, max or levelplay are better bets. Match the pricing model and lock-in level to how your team actually works, not the feature list.
quick_take: OpenX is for programmatic ad exchange and supply-side platform; Fyber is for mobile ad mediation and in-app bidding platform; decide based on whether you can accept sales-call pricing.
verdict_a: Choose OpenX if keeping the configuration portable matters and the work maps to programmatic ad exchange and supply-side platform for publishers.
verdict_b: Choose Fyber if you can tolerate some setup friction and the work lines up with mobile ad mediation and in-app bidding platform (Digital Turbine).

faq.a: Neither OpenX nor Fyber publishes entry pricing that lets us compare directly — OpenX uses a custom model and Fyber uses a custom model. Get quotes from both before assuming one is cheaper.

faq.q: Can I migrate from OpenX to Fyber?
faq.a: Moving from OpenX to Fyber is feasible, but Fyber is rated high lock-in — expect your integrations and workflows to need rework to match its model. Budget for a parallel-run period rather than a hard cutover.

Both OpenX and Fyber rate the same on developer experience (3/5). The decision on DX then comes down to taste — which CLI, UI, or workflow matches your team's habits. A short side-by-side trial is the quickest way to tell.

faq.q: Is Fyber a good alternative to OpenX?
faq.a: Yes — Fyber is a reasonable alternative to OpenX for large publishers wanting another demand source in their header bidding wrapper.. The practical differences are custom-vs-custom billing and low-vs-high lock-in. If those fit your constraints better, treat Fyber as a credible swap.
